title: Share of GDP per capita spent by the government on each primary school student vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: GDP per capita data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Share of GDP per capita spent by the government on each primary school student vs. GDP per capita, 2008
GDP per capita data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between national wealth and education investment for 2008, plotting government expenditure per primary student as a percentage of GDP per capita against GDP per capita in international dollars. The horizontal axis uses a logarithmic scale to display income levels ranging from under $2,000 to over $100,000, while the vertical axis measures spending intensity from 0% to over 40%. Data points are color-coded by region, distinguishing North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. The distribution shows no clear linear relationship between a country's wealth and the share of that wealth spent on each student. Serbia occupies the highest position on the vertical axis, spending over 40% of its GDP per capita on each primary student, followed by Djibouti at roughly 38% and Albania near 35%. In contrast, Kazakhstan sits at the bottom of the chart with a share close to 0%, while Bermuda and Saint Kitts and Nevis fall below 10%. Among high-income nations with GDP per capita exceeding $50,000, spending shares vary significantly: Cyprus allocates roughly 32%, South Korea around 29%, and Australia approximately 21%, whereas Hong Kong and Bermuda allocate less than 15%. Lower-income countries also display wide variation; Burundi and Niger spend between 10% and 15%, while Rwanda and DR Congo spend under 10%. The figures exclude international aviation and GDP per capita is expressed in international-$ at 2021 prices. The data shows that high national income does not automatically correspond to a higher proportion of that income being directed toward individual primary students.
